title: Garibaldi biscuits
subtitle: Victorian currant-sandwiched biscuits, thin and crisp
excerpt: A Victorian biscuit with a dense layer of currants pressed between two thin sheets of dough. Roll, scatter, fold, and roll again. The currants seal inside and caramelise slightly in the oven. Makes about twenty-four biscuits.
sourceNotes: The Garibaldi biscuit was named in honour of the Italian general Giuseppe Garibaldi, who visited England in 1854. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: small dried-fruit biscuits of this style appear among the household biscuit entries. The sandwich construction with currants pressed between two thin layers of plain dough is the defining feature documented across Victorian household manuals.

content.text: The Garibaldi is a biscuit with a clear rationale: plain dough exists only to hold the currants, and the currants are the whole point. The thin sheets bake to a crisp, pale gold around a dense, chewy middle. Roll them as thin as you dare. Thick Garibaldis are not the thing.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}} and {{caster-sugar}} until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the beaten {{eggs}} and mix with a fork until a smooth dough forms. If it is too dry, add the {{whole-milk}} a splash at a time. Divide the dough into two equal pieces.

On a lightly floured surface, roll one piece of dough into a rectangle about 3 mm thick. Scatter the {{currants}} evenly over the surface, pressing them gently into the dough. Roll the second piece to the same size and lay it on top. Press lightly with the rolling pin to seal.
the surface all over with a fork. Cut into rectangles about 5 × 3 cm. Transfer to a lined baking tray.

Bake for 12 to 14 minutes, until pale gold at the edges and set on the surface. The currants will colour and catch slightly at the edges, which is correct. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. They crisp further as they cool.
